coredata.dc:title: Catalytic and peroxidase-like activity of carbon based-AuPd bimetallic nanocomposite produced using carbon dots as the reductant
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Liuqing
Liu, Xiaoying
Lu, Qiujun
Huang, Na
Liu, Meiling
Zhang, Youyu
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: In this report, carbon-based AuPd bimetallic nanocomposite (AuPd/C NC) was synthesized using carbon dots (C-dots) as the reducing agent and stabilizer by a simple green sequential reduction strategy, without adding other agents. The as synthesized AuPd/C NC showed good catalytic activity and peroxidase-like property. The structure and morphology of these nanoparticles were clearly characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The AuPd/C NC catalyst exhibits noticeably higher catalytic activity than Pd and Au nanoparticles in catalysis reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). Moreover, based on the high peroxidase-like property of AuPd/C NC, a new colorimetric detection method for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been designed using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl-benzidine (TMB) as the substrate, which provides a simple and sensitive means to detect H2O2 in wide linear range of 5 μM–500 μM and 500 μM–4 mM with low detection limit of 1.6 μM (S/N = 3). Therefore, the facile synthesis strategy for bimetallic nanoparticles by the mild reductant of carbon dot will provide some new thoughts for preparing of carbon-based metal nanomaterials and expand their application in catalysis and analytical chemistry areas.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
AuPd/C nanocomposite
Sequential reduction
Catalytic activity
Peroxidase-like activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the AuPd/C NC
Catalytic property of AuPd/C NC bimetallic nanoparticles
Characterization the peroxidase-like activity of AuPd/C NC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of C-dots (A), Au NPs (B), Pd NPs (C), AuPd/C NC (D, E). Size distribution histograms of AuPd/C NC (F).
(A) XPS survey spectrum of AuPd/C NC, high resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1s, (C) Pd 3d and (D) Au 4f in the AuPd/C NC.
Time-dependent of UV–Vis absorption spectra for the reduction of 90 μM 4-nitrophenol with C-dots (A), Au (B), Pd (C) and AuPd/C NC(D) in the presence of 1.8 mM NaBH4.

Plot of ln(At/A0) versus reaction time for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol with C-dots (a), Au (b), Pd (c) and AuPd/C NC (d) as the catalyst. Here, At is the absorbance of 4-nitrophenol at reaction time t and A0 is the original absorbance in Fig. 3.

The effects of the AuPd/C NC in the reaction systems of (a) TMB, AuPd/C NC and H2O2, (b) TMB and H2O2, (c) TMB and AuPd/C NC. Reaction conditions: the solutions were incubated in 0.2 M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.0) at 37 °C for 10 min. Inset shows the color change of the related systems. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–Vis spectra for the mixed solutions of TMB, AuPd/C NC and various concentrations of H2O2 in 0.2 M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.0) at 37 °C for 10 min. The TMB concentration was 20 μM and 10 μL AuPd/C NC solution was used. Inset: the corresponding images of the colored products. (B) Dependence of the absorbance at 652 nm on the concentration of H2O2 from 5 μM to 4 mM. Inset: the corresponding linear calibration plot. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Illustration of the preparation and application of AuPd/C NC.
Comparisons of different materials with peroxidase-like activity for determination of H2O2.

head.ce:title: Catalytic and peroxidase-like activity of carbon based-AuPd bimetallic nanocomposite produced using carbon dots as the reductant

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Science and Technological Innovation Platform, Hunan Agricultural University, Hunan, Changsha 410128, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this report, carbon-based AuPd bimetallic nanocomposite (AuPd/C NC) was synthesized using carbon dots (C-dots) as the reducing agent and stabilizer by a simple green sequential reduction strategy, without adding other agents. The as synthesized AuPd/C NC showed good catalytic activity and peroxidase-like property. The structure and morphology of these nanoparticles were clearly characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The AuPd/C NC catalyst exhibits noticeably higher catalytic activity than Pd and Au nanoparticles in catalysis reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). Moreover, based on the high peroxidase-like property of AuPd/C NC, a new colorimetric detection method for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been designed using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl-benzidine (TMB) as the substrate, which provides a simple and sensitive means to detect H2O2 in wide linear range of 5 μM–500 μM and 500 μM–4 mM with low detection limit of 1.6 μM (S/N = 3). Therefore, the facile synthesis strategy for bimetallic nanoparticles by the mild reductant of carbon dot will provide some new thoughts for preparing of carbon-based metal nanomaterials and expand their application in catalysis and analytical chemistry areas.

ce:section.ce:para: Noble metal nanomaterials, such as Au, Ag, Pt and Pd, as one of the most important materials, have received increasing research interest in recent decades because of their wide applications in catalysis, sensing, electrochemical energy storage, biomedical diagnosis, therapy, etc [1]. Among them, Au nanoparticles possess rapid electron transfer, excellent chemical stability [2], efficient electrocatalytic properties and good biocompatibility [3]. Bimetallic nanoparticles, composed of two chemically distinct metals with core-shell, intermixed or alloyed nanostructures have aroused extensive interests because of their unique optical, electronic, magnetic and especially catalytic properties [3–7]. Previous works have shown that Pd-based bimetallic catalysts display many advantages for their unique physicochemical properties and enhanced catalytic performance [8]. Particularly with regard to their catalytic performance, they often show superior catalytic activity, selectivity or stability than the homologous monometallic counterparts, resulting from the synergistic effects of both components [5–7,9]. Especially, the Au-Pd bimetallic has drawn wide attention, as it exhibits high activities towards a huge number of useful chemical reactions such as CO oxidation [10–13], methanol oxidation [14], formic acid dehydrogenation [8,11,15] and alcohols or hydrocarbons selective oxidation [5,13,16,17]. Recently, AuPd has also been reported to be effectively employed in direct ethanol fuel cells [13], photocatalytic [18] and electrocatalytic [19,20].

It is reported that the structure, morphology, properties and catalytic activity of bimetallic nanoparticles can be adjusted by using different reductant, stabilizer and surfactant. Li et al. prepared the worm-like Au-Pd nanostructures by using polyethylene glycol monooleyl ether as the growth-directing agent, stabilizing agent and mild reducing agent [6]. It is reported that it is important to choose proper reducing agents and stabilizers in preparing of bimetallic nanoparticles owing to the special require of the morphology and properties of the nanoparticles. Generally, sodium borohydride [21,22], ascorbic acid [21–23] and cetyltrimethylammonium chloride [24], formaldehyde [25] were usually used as the reductant in synthesis of bimetallic nanoparticles. Normally, in the process of preparing bimetallic nanoparticles via the sequential reduction method, two different reducing agents were usually used. Although the particle size and morphology can be easily controlled depending on the desired applications by this method, it would easily introduce some by-products. For example, Tesfalidet Balcha et al. prepared AuPd core-shell nanomaterial by this method [21]. The HAuCl4 was reduced by NaBH4 to form Au seed. After the process of crystal growth, K2PdCl4 was reduced by ascorbic acid. Pd attached to the Au crystal surface, resulting in the formation of AuPd core-shell nanoparticles. The method is effective. However, the nanoparticles had to dialyze over night to remove the excess salts and ascorbic acid before use and at the same time the AuPd was suffered from aggregation. Zhou et al. prepared AuPd core-shell structure with the sequential reduction method by using NaBH4 and ascorbic acid [22]. Moreover, the obtained nanoparticles which were reduced by these reductants were easy to aggregate. In order to prevent bimetallic nanoparticles from aggregation, additional appropriate surfactants are usually utilized in the preparation of bimetallic nanoparticles. Krishna Kanta Haldar et al. synthesized Au/Ag bimetallic core-shell nanoparticles with polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) [26]. Although this method could solve the problem of aggregation in certain extent, the synthesis process requires two kinds of reducing agents and another stabilizer, which was complicated and could introduce some by-products. To select a by-product free approach, it is better to develop an alternative strategy with new reductants which can be also used as the stabilization agents to prepare the nanomaterials. Traditionally, there are some materials that can be acted as reducing agent and surfactant, such as octadecylamine (OTA) [27,28] and polyethylene glycol monooleyl ether [6]. However, to our knowledge, it is not common to use carbon-based nanomaterials as the reductant and stabilization agents to prepare bimetallic nanoparticles.

Among the carbon based nanomaterials, carbon nanodots (C-dots) which is a new kind of carbon nanomaterial consisting of graphitic structure or amorphous carbon core and carbonaceous surfaces with rich oxygen-containing groups have attracted considerable attention in recent years [29–32]. C-dots attracted extensive studies due to their promising applications in bioimaging, sensors, optoelectronic devices and catalysis areas [29–32]. To our knowledge, C-dots are quasi-spherical nanoparticles and they have good properties such as solubility, desirable biocompatibility and nontoxicity [29]. Li et al. fabricated an ionic liquid-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanodots (IL-CDs) sensor which displayed favorable electrocatalysis toward the reduction of O2 and H2O2 [33]. C-dots can also act as excellent electron acceptors and electron donors [31,34], and have promising potential to be oxidized or act as reduce agents. Owing to the special properties of carbon dots, it may be used as the reductant and stabilizer to prepare bimetallic nanomaterials and prevent bimetallic nanoparticles from aggregation and improv the properties of the materials.

In this paper, a simple green sequential reduction method for synthesis of AuPd was developed, only with the assistance of C-dots. Compared with other reducing agents, such as sodium borohydride and hydrazine, C-dots had good biocompatibility and were environment friendly. Here, the C-dots were used as the reductant for direct synthesis of AuPd as well as the stabilizing agent and substrate for immobilization of AuPd. Therefore, there were no toxic substances and pollutants produced in the process of synthesis and this method is simple and green. The as synthesized AuPd/C NC showed excellent stability and catalytic activity. The as proposed carbon-based AuPd bimetallic nanoparticles exhibit excellent catalytic activity in reduction of 4-nitrophenol, and have peroxidase-like activity, which is far superior to those of the monometallic counterparts.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O) was obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl-benzidine (TMB) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Huihong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Palladium chloride (PdCl2) was purchased from Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrogen peroxide (30% H2O2) and acetic acid (CH3COOH) were obtained from Shanghai Guoyao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) was obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium acetate anhydrous (CH3COONa) was purchased from Shanghai Shanfu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Acetate buffer solution (HAc-NaAc) was prepared by 0.2 M HAc and 0.2 M NaAc. All other reagents were of analytical grades and used as received. Milli-Q ultrapure water was used in all experiments. The experiments were performed at room temperature.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were collected on a JEM-1230 transmission electronic microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out on K-Alpha 1063 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, England). UV–Vis spectra were conducted on the UV-2450 spectra-photometer (Shimadzu, Japan). FT-IR spectra were collected on a pressed pellet with KBr in the transmission mode on a Nexus-670 Fourier transform-infrared spectrophotometer (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA).

The hydroxyl-rich C-dots were synthesized by one-pot electrochemical carbonization of ethylene glycol as we reported [29]. Briefly, ethylene glycol and sodium hydroxide were mixed and used as electrolytes; platinum sheets were used as the positive and the negative electrodes. The C-dots were synthesized by electrolyzing the electrolytes under 30 V (DC) for 40 min. After electrolysis, the electrolyte solution was neutralized with hydrochloric acid, and then dialyzed (Mw 1000) against water for two days. The supernatant was centrifugated to remove the residual components.

The AuPd/C NC was prepared as described in Scheme 1 . AuPd/C NC was synthesized using C-dots as the reductant, HAuCl4 and PdCl2 as the precursor. In brief, 300 μL of the as prepared C-dots solution was diluted to 600 μL with ultrapure water, then 20 μL of 10 mM HAuCl4 was added into the C-dots solution by simply mixing and the color of the mixture changed from yellow to wine red immediately (within 30 s). The solution was incubated for 3 h at 30 °C to form Au seed. Then 50 μL C-dots solution was added into the above solution. After 5 min, 60 μL of 10 mM PdCl2 was added into the obtained solution within 10 min and the color of the solution gradually turned from wine red to brownish black. AuPd/C NC was obtained without using any additional reductant and stabilizing agent. For comparison, Au NPs, Pd NPs were prepared using the same process. The C-dots, Au NPs, Pd NPs and AuPd/C NC were stored at 4 °C in refrigerator when not in use.

The activity of the AuPd/C NC was evaluated by the catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol in the presence of NaBH4, and the reaction was carried out in a quartz cuvette at room temperature. The reaction procedures were as follows: 200 μL of 20 mM NaBH4 was mixed with 20 μL of 10 mM 4-nitrophenol, and then 2.0 mL of deionized water was added into the above solution. Then, the as-prepared AuPd/C NC (5 μL) was injected into the above solution. Immediately after that, the solution was transferred to a standard quartz cell and the UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded at fixed time intervals. For comparison, the catalysis properties of Au and Pd nanoparticles were also studied by the same procedures.

To investigate the peroxidase-like activity of the AuPd/C NC toward H2O2, TMB was chosen as the substrate. In a typical experiment, H2O2 (10 μL, 50 mM) was mixed with 930 μL HAc-NaAc (0.2 M, pH 4.0), TMB (50 μL, 10 mM) and AuPd/C NC (10 μL) for 10 min of incubation at 37 °C. In detection of H2O2, various concentrations of H2O2 were added to initiate the reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the AuPd/C NC
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of nanoparticles was firstly confirmed by UV. Fig. S1A shows the typical UV–Vis spectra of C-dots (a), Au NPs (b), Pd NPs (c) and AuPd/C NC (d). The as-synthesized C-dots have a strong absorption peak at 258 nm, which is typically attributed to the π-π* transition [29,31]. After addition in the solution of AuCl4 −, the solution was suddenly changed to wine red, which resulted from the reduction of AuCl4 − into Au NPs by C-dots. The remaining C-dots can be used as stabilizing agent which may prevent the aggregation of Au NPs. The strong absorption band at 527 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum shown in Fig. S1b is the characteristic peak of the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band for Au NPs resulted from reduction of AuCl4 − solution by C-dots. And it was found that the existence of C-dots did not change the spectrum of Au NPs. In addition, no color change and aggregation for the Au NPs were observed after storage for more than four months. And in the solution, only Au NPs and C-dots existed in the system and other small molecules such as ethylene glycol were removed by dialyzing. It indicated that C-dots could act as an excellent reductant and stabilizer for reducing gold ions and stabilizing the nanoparticles. While for the spectra of Pd NPs, there was not any clear SPR signal in the absorption spectrum as shown in Fig. S1c. The disappearance of the surface plasmon characteristic peak of Au NPs at 527 nm in Fig. S1d was consistent with the formation of Pd rich surfaces upon reduction of the Pd salts onto the Au seeds. It is considered that the disappearance of the plasmonic band is the characteristic of changes in morphology and chemical components of the nanoparticles. Therefore, this result also suggested the formation of bimetallic nanoparticles. The solution was also stable when storage, and this stability may be due to the interaction of the residual hydroxyl groups of C-dots with the bimetallic nanoparticles through hydrogen bonding to protect them from aggregation [29].

Fig. S1B shows the typical IR spectra of C-dots (a), Au NPs (b), Pd NPs (c) and AuPd/C NC (d). The characteristic peak of hydroxyl groups (OH) at 3428 cm−1 and carbonyl groups (CO) at 1705 cm−1 were observed in the spectrum of C-dots [29,31]. Compared with C-dots, the relative intensity of hydroxyl groups (OH) decreased and the intensity of carbonyl groups (CO, 1712 cm−1) increased after reaction with HAuCl4, indicating that hydroxyl groups (OH) on the surface of C-dots may play an important role in reducing of HAuCl4 into AuNPs. Similarly, the main characteristic peak in the spectra of Pd NPs (c) and AuPd/C NC (d) are similar to that of AuNPs.

The morphologies and microstructures of C-dots (A), Au NPs (B), Pd NPs (C) and AuPd/C NC (D) were investigated by TEM. Fig. 1 A shows that the C-dots are of low multiple dispersity with the diameters ranging in 2–4 nm. The HRTEM image of one nanoparticle (Fig. 1A, inset) shows a crystalline structure with the lattice spacing of 0.200 nm, which can be attributed to the (102) diffraction planes of graphitic (sp2) carbon [29,31]. The as-synthesized Au NPs showed good dispersion as shown in Fig. 1B, and the Au NPs is nearly spherical with diameters ranging from 12 to 14 nm. The clear lattice spacing of 0.240 nm in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1B, inset) was found which can be attributed to the (111) lattice space of metallic Au NPs [29,31]. Moreover, Au NPs was surrounded by carbon dots which may prevent the aggregation of Au NPs. Fig. 1C shows the Pd NPs with the diameters ranging in 8–10 nm. The HRTEM image of one nanoparticle (Fig. 1C, inset) shows a crystalline structure with the lattice spacing of 0.230 nm, which can be attributed to the (111) diffraction planes of Pd NPs [4,35,36]. Fig. 1D shows that the AuPd/C NC is of good dispersity and the average diameter of the AuPd/C NC was determined to be 15.7 ± 1.1 nm (Fig. 1F). The HRTEM image of one nanoparticle (Fig. 1E) shows that Au NPs surrounded by Pd NPs. The lattice distance of 0.238 nm corresponds to (111) lattice space of metallic Au NPs. The lattice distance of 0.205 nm corresponds to (002) [4] facets of face-centered cubic Pd NPs, whereas the lattice distance of 0.231 nm corresponds to (111) facets of Pd NPs [4,35,36].

XPS measurements are performed to determine the composition and chemical state of the as-prepared AuPd/C NC. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2
A) reveals the existence of Au, Pd, C, and O elements. The C1s (Fig. 2B) shows three distinct peaks at 284.8, 286.3 and 288.6 eV. The first peak can be ascribed to carbon species adsorbed on the surface of AuPd [37]; the second and the third binding energy peak belong to the oxygen containing groups of CO [15,38] and CO [38] groups, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 peaks in the spectrum of AuPd/C NC located at 84.3 and 88.0 eV [15,18,37–40], respectively, suggesting the formation of metallic Au (0) in the nanocomposite [38]. No obvious peak for Au3+ could be found, which also confirms the complete reduction of Au3+ to Au (0) in our designed process. The XPS spectrum of Pd 3d (Fig. 2D) shows that the chemical states of Pd on the surface of AuPd/C NC has two states for Pd species. The Pd 3d5/2 peak at 335.4 eV and Pd 3d3/2 peak at 341.0 eV are attributed to the metallic of Pd0 [15,18,37–40]. While the binding energy peaks shown at 337.7 and 343.1 eV are originated from Pd2+ [15,38]. This demonstrated that Pd2+ and Au3+ ions are reduced by C-dots to form Pd0 and Au0 without any additional reducing agents.

The mechanism of synthesizing AuPd/C NC by using C-dots as the reducing agent was as follows: the hydroxyl groups (OH) on the surface of C-dots may reduce of HAuCl4 into Au NPs and at the same time, the OH convert itself into carbonyl groups (CO). The remaining C-dots can be used as stabilizing agent preventing the aggregation of Au NPs. Similarly, Pd2+ ions were reduced to Pd NPs by C-dots. This can be confirmed by the following experiment (Fig. S3): KMnO4 (H+) solution was dropped into the C-dots solution and the hydroxyl groups (OH) on the surface of C-dots will be oxidized by KMnO4, which can restrain the reduction of the HAuCl4 owing to the disappearance the OH on C-dots surface. The purple color of KMnO4 was disappeared and the yellow color of C-dots was gradually faded. There was not any color change when adding HAuCl4 in the treated C-dots solution even after a period of time. Therefore, the HAuCl4 cannot be reduced by the KMnO4 (H+) treated C-dots and this result supported our suppose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic property of AuPd/C NC bimetallic nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the catalytic activity of the prepared AuPd/C NC, the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) in the presence of excess NaBH4 was chosen as a model system. Scheme 1 shows the catalytic process of 4-nitrophenol reduction. The reduction reaction was monitored by UV–Vis spectroscopy and the results are shown in Fig. 3D. After addition of NaBH4 as the reducing agent into 4-nitrophenol aqueous solution, the pale yellow color of the 4-NP solution turned to yellow rapidly, and a strong absorption peak emerged in the UV–Vis spectrum at 400 nm, due to the formation of 4-nitrophenol anions under basic conditions. In the absence of the metallic nanoparticles as the catalyst, the reduction reaction does not proceed. When AuPd/C NC was introduced into the mixed solution, the intensity of this peak gradually decreased as the reaction proceeded. The peak intensity of 4-nitrophenol at 400 nm decreased rapidly with time and a concomitant increase of the absorption peak at 300 nm which was due to the reduction product of 4-aminophenol. When the AuPd/C NC were used as the catalyst, the synergistic catalytic effects of Au and Pd produced a fast and complete conversion of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol within 6 min (Fig. 3D). For comparison, the reduction of 4-nitrophenol in the systems of C-dots (A), Au NPs (B), Pd NPs (C) were also investigated under the same conditions and the reaction time were 22, 15 and 10 min, respectively (Fig. 3A–C). In addition, the reaction time of AuPd/C NC was shorter than those of the systems of C-dots, Au NPs and Pd NPs. It indicates that the AuPd/C NC has higher catalytic activity than Pd NPs and Au NPs. Therefore, AuPd/C NC has a better catalytic performance to the reduction of 4-nitrophenol.

The kinetics of the reduction of 4-nitrophenol in the presence of different catalysts was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 4. Since the concentration of NaBH4 is very high compared with that of 4-NP, the reactions followed the pseudo-first order reaction kinetics. Thus, the pseudo-first order rate constant of the reaction can be calculated from the following equation: ln(At/A0) = kt [26,41,42], where At is the absorbance of 4-NP at 400 nm at time t, and A0 is the initial absorbance at 400 nm of 4-NP. The plot of ln(At/A0) vs. t for the reaction of 4-NP catalyzed by AuPd/C NC is linear as shown in Fig. 4. The corresponding rate constant k is the slope of the plot with the value of 0.7227 min−1. By comparison, the rate constant k in the systems of C-dots (A), Au NPs (B), Pd NPs (C) were 0.1376 min−1, 0.1923 min−1 and 0.2456 min−1, respectively (Fig. 4a–c). Obviously, they were much smaller than that of AuPd/C NC. Therefore, due to the synergistic catalytic effect of the components of Au and Pd, the catalytic performance of AuPd/C NC is much better than the individual component and their overlay effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization the peroxidase-like activity of AuPd/C NC
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the peroxidase-like activity of AuPd/C NC, TMB was chosen as the substrate, which can be oxidized by H2O2 to a colored product in the catalysis of the peroxidase mimetics (as shown in the following equation) [43]
H 2
O 2
+ TMB
→
catalyst
H 2
O
+ oxidized
TMB

The catalytic oxidation of the peroxidase substrate TMB was tested in the presence of AuPd/C NC and H2O2. AuPd/C NC shows excellent catalytic performance in oxidation of TMB. In a typical TMB oxidation process, the color of the transparent substrate (TMB + buffer solution) will turn to blue after the addition of AuPd/C NC and H2O2, and the maximum absorbance at 652 nm will gradually increase during this reaction. Therefore, UV–Vis absorption spectra were employed to evaluate the peroxidase activity of the AuPd/C NC. Fig. 5 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the different test solutions. Compared with the absorption spectrum of the mixture of the AuPd/C NC and the TMB solution (c), weak absorptions at 370 nm and 652 nm were observed in the case of TMB and H2O2 (b), which indicated weak oxidation of TMB by H2O2. Noticeable color change in the solution also can be observed (inset in Fig. 5) and much stronger absorbance can be found in the presence of the mixture of AuPd/C NC, TMB and H2O2 solution (a). It indicated that the reaction of TMB with H2O2 was greatly accelerated by the AuPd/C NC. All of the results confirmed that the as-prepared AuPd/C NC exhibited an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, which was similar to that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [45].

It is well known that enzymatic activity is dependent upon the substrate and the reaction conditions. Therefore, the catalytic activity of the AuPd/C NC may be affected by pH and temperature. As shown in Fig. S2, the UV–Vis absorbance intensity at 652 nm increased with pH in the range of 3.0–4.0, and then declined with increasing the pH. The absorbance intensity at 652 nm increased with temperature range from 20 to 37 °C, and then declined. Hence, the catalytic activity was best in pH 4.0 HAc-NaAc buffer at 37 °C.

The detection of H2O2 was detected under the optimized condition, in which the concentrations of H2O2 increased. The color of TMB was dependent on H2O2 concentrations which catalyzed by the as-synthesized AuPd/C NC. Additionally, the absorbance at 652 nm increased with increasing H2O2 concentrations (Fig. 6 A). This indicates that the absorbance change may be used for the detection of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 6B, the absorbance at 652 nm was proportional to H2O2 concentrations, the linear detection range of H2O2 was 5 μM–500 μM and 500 μM–4 mM, the detection limit was estimated to be 1.6 μM (S/N = 3). Table 1 shows the comparison of the detection of H2O2 by AuPd/C NC in this work with other published different catalysts with peroxidase-like property. The results showed that the as prepared material had better linear range and lower detection limit.

In summary, a facile and green synthesis method for carbon based AuPd bimetallic nanoparticles with the assistance of C-dots as the stabilizing agent and mild reducing agent through a sequential reduction strategy was proposed. This method is simple and the carbon based AuPd bimetallic nanoparticles possess high catalytic activity and good peroxidase-like property. The AuPd/C NC shows higher catalytic activity for the reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP than the homologous monometallic counterparts. The results also confirmed that the as-prepared AuPd/C NC exhibited an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and can be utilized for detection of H2O2. Therefore, C-dots can be used as an efficient reductant and stabilizing agent for fast synthesis of carbon-based noble metal nanomaterials. This obtained carbon-based noble metal nanomaterials have great prospect and may be implicated in bioimaging, sensors, optoelectronic devices and catalysis areas.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305042, 21375037), Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (14B116), Science and Technology Department (14JJ4030), the Opening Fund of state key laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (2013017), key laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistant Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle (ST201422007), the Construct Program of the Key Discipline in Hunan Province and the Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
The Supporting Information is available free of charge on the http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.04.041.

UV and FT-IR spectra of C-dots, Au, Pd and AuPd/C NC, the effect of pH, temperature on peroxidase-like activity of the AuPd/C NC, UV spectra of C-dots, C-dots + KMnO4 (H+), C-dots + KMnO4 (H+) + HAuCl4 and C-dots + HAuCl4 are presented in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.04.041.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering ordered and nonordered porous noble metal nanostructures: synthesis, assembly, and their applications in electrochemistry

Simple synthesis of worm-like Au-Pd nanostructures supported on reduced graphene oxide for highly sensitive detection of nitrite

Plasmon-enhanced formic acid dehydrogenation using anisotropic Pd-Au nanorods studied at the single-particle level

Diatomic steps in Pt(997) surfaces are better catalysts than monatomic steps for the CO oxidation reaction near atmospheric pressure
Effect of carbon supports on electrocatalytic reactivity of Au-Pd core-shell nanoparticles
Electrocatalytic properties of strained Pd nanoshells at Au nanostructures: CO and HCOOH oxidation
Interactions of hydrogen and carbon monoxide on Pd-Au bimetallic surfaces
Theoretical studies on the synergetic effects of Au-Pd bimetallic catalysts in the selective oxidation of methanol

Spongelike nanoporous Pd and Pd/Au structures: facile synthesis and enhanced electrocatalytic activity
Functionalization of Au/TiO2 plasmonic photocatalysts with Pd by formation of a core-shell structure for effective dechlorination of chlorobenzene under irradiation of visible light
Remarkable enhancement of electrocatalytic activity by tuning the interface of Pd-Au bimetallic nanoparticle tubes
CO2 electroreduction at bare and Cu-decorated Pd pseudomorphic layers: catalyst tuning by controlled and indirect supporting onto Au(111)

Hydroxyl-rich C-dots synthesized by a one-pot method and their application in the preparation of noble metal nanoparticles

Simple and sensitive fluorescent and electrochemical trinitrotoluene sensors based on aqueous carbon dots
Ionic liquid-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanodots and their applications in electrocatalysis, biosensing, and cell imaging
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots

AuPd bimetallic nanoparticles decorated graphitic carbon nitride for highly efficient reduction of water to H2 under visible light irradiation
Au@Pd core-shell nanoparticles-decorated reduced graphene oxide: a highly sensitive and selective platform for electrochemical detection of hydrazine
A facile method to synthesize supported Pd-Au nanoparticles using graphene oxide as the reductant and their extremely high electrocatalytic activity for the electrooxidation of methanol and ethanol
Highly active Au-Pd nanoparticles supported on three-dimensional graphene-carbon nanotube hybrid for selective oxidation of methanol to methyl formate
Kinetic analysis of catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol by metallic nanoparticles immobilized in spherical polyelectrolyte brushes
